turns.content: got roped into the battalion chiefs conference again. need help figuring out the travel.
Sure, I can help with that. Where and when is it?
San Antonio. September 15 through 17. two and a half days of guys reading slides at you while you drink burnt coffee in a ballroom. only upside is I get out of the office for a few days.

San Antonio in mid-September will still be warm — probably mid 90s. For flights from Philly, you're looking at either direct on American through DFW or connecting through Houston on United. Direct flights are usually around 3.5 to 4 hours. When do you want to fly in — the night before on the 14th?
yeah the 14th makes sense. conference starts early on the 15th. registration at 0700. I'd rather get in the night before than scramble with a morning flight.
Smart. And fly back on the 17th after the conference wraps, or the morning of the 18th?
17th if I can. it ends around 1600. I'll grab a late flight home. I don't need an extra night in a hotel if I can avoid it.

Makes sense. There are usually evening flights out of SAT back to PHL in the 7-8pm range. That gives you time to get from the venue to the airport. Is the department covering flights and hotel, or is this out of pocket?

department covers the hotel and registration. flights I have to book through their travel system but they reimburse. it's a whole process. last time I went to one of these I had to submit receipts three times before they processed it.
Government reimbursement at its finest. Do you know which hotel the conference is at? Sometimes they have a block rate that's easier to get approved through the department system.
Marriott Riverwalk I think. that's where it was two years ago. haven't gotten the official logistics email yet but it's usually the same spot.

The Marriott Riverwalk is a solid spot — right on the River Walk so you can walk to restaurants and bars without needing a car. If they have a conference block rate, jump on it early. Those rooms fill up fast, especially in September when it's still tourist season.

good call. I'll book as soon as the email comes through. listen the conference itself is mostly a grind. lot of administrative stuff, policy updates, some vendor presentations. but they do a couple sessions on incident command and large-scale response that are actually worth sitting through.

Those hands-on IC sessions are usually the highlight at these things. The vendor floor can be worth a pass through too if you're looking at equipment or tech. Anything specific you're hoping to get out of it this year?

not really. I go because it's expected. the networking is useful I guess. you see guys from other departments, swap stories, find out what's working in other cities. but three days is a lot of sitting for a guy who's used to being on his feet.

Three days of conference chairs after 30 years of firehouses — I can see why that's a tough adjustment. At least San Antonio has good food. The River Walk has some solid Tex-Mex if you need an escape from the hotel banquet chicken.
now you're talking. alright I'll wait for the logistics email and book the flights. appreciate the help thinking it through.
Anytime. Book early, grab the block rate, and find the best barbecue spot on the River Walk. You'll survive three days of PowerPoints.
